Journal.Title: Journal of health, population, and nutrition

Article.ArticleTitle: Magnitude of undernutrition and associated factors among adult tuberculosis patients in West Shoa Zone, Oromia Region, Ethiopia: a facility-based cross-sectional study.

Abstract.AbstractText: There is a growing realization that undernutrition imposes a significant risk for Tuberculosis (TB) burden, particularly in resource-limited settings. Despite this rationalization, the association between undernutrition and tuberculosis is not explicitly demonstrated in the study settings. Thus, the aimed to evaluate the magnitude of undernutrition and associated factors among adult tuberculosis patients in West Shoa Zone, Oromia Region, Ethiopia, 2023.

We conducted a facility-based cross-sectional study among 397 participants. A systematic random sampling technique was used from 28th April to 20th May 2023. Data were obtained by structured questionnaires and anthropometric measurements. Multivariable logistic regression was employed to identify factors independently associated with undernutrition among adult tuberculosis patients. Adjusted Odds Ratios (AOR) and 95% confidence interval (CI) were obtained, with the significance level set at a p-value < 0.05.

The overall magnitude of undernutrition was 38.1% (95%CI = 33.27, 42.92). Multivariable logistic analysis revealed that undernutrition was significantly influenced by young age between 18 and 24 years [AOR = 3.85, 95% CI (1.92,7.74)] and 25-34 years [AOR = 2.00, 95% CI (1.32,3.91)], having no formal education [AOR = 2.90, 95% CI (1.41,5.95)], low household wealth index [AOR = 1.79,95% CI (1.23,3.11)], alcohol consumption [AOR = 1.98, 95% CI (1.24,3.43)], having more than size 5 family members in the households [AOR = 1.73, 95% CI (1.26,2.82)], and lack of nutritional care and support [AOR = 1.84, 95% CI (1.39,3.09)].

The study found a high prevalence of undernutrition (38.1%; 95% CI: 33.27-42.92%) among adult TB patients. Younger age, no formal educational status, low household wealth index, and alcohol consumption, larger family size, and having no nutritional care and support were significant predictors of undernutrition. These findings highlight the need for integrated nutritional assessment and support and socioeconomic interventions for TB patients during treatment.

AffiliationInfo.Affiliation: West Shoa Zonal health office Ambo Town, Ambo Town, Oromia Region, Ethiopia.

West Shoa Zonal health office Ambo Town, Ambo Town, Oromia Region, Ethiopia.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: Declarations. Ethics approval and consent to participate: The Ethical Review Board of Ambo University’s College of Health Sciences and Referral Hospital evaluated and approved this study (Ref. No. AU/PGC/696/2015). The study was conducted using the Declaration of Helsinki’s guiding principles [43]. Subsequently, a formal letter of collaboration was acquired from the health office located in West Shoa. At last, all literate participants were asked to provide their informed written consent; participants’ verbal consent had been obtained for those unable to read and write following a thorough explanation about the study’s objectives, potential risks and benefits, participant rights, and confidentiality. For illiterate participants, the consent form was read aloud by a trained data collectors in a language they understood. A neutral third-party witness was present to ensure ethical conduct and comprehension. Participants verbally confirmed their understanding and willingness to participate, and their verbal consent was documented in the presence of the witness. Participants were also provided with a copy of the consent form for their records. The opportunity to ask any questions they may have had regarding the study, as well as the freedom to decline or stop participating at any moment, were provided to the study participants. Every participant’s personal information is kept anonymous, and their privacy is guaranteed for the entire length of the study. Consent for publication: Each author made revisions to the text and provided approval before the paper was submitted for publication. Competing interests: The authors declare no competing interests.
